title: Bridge Paper: Heart-brain axis dysregulation in PTSD mice: Vagal-mediated insular cortex hyper
author: Frontier Science / Hermes T2

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Body-energy** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge thesis:** Spiritus as interoceptive inference — heart-brain coupling, energy metabolism as felt presence.
**Phase context:** Interoception, heart-brain, vagus nerve, energy metabolism, spiritus

Paper: Heart-brain axis dysregulation in PTSD mice: Vagal-mediated insular cortex hyperactivity and its reversal by propranolol.
DOI: 10.1016/j.ejphar.2026.178961
Abstract: BACKGROUND: While abnormal brain connectivity is central to PTSD, sympathetically-driven cardiac dysfunction also occurs. Given the established role of cardiac modulation of brain function and its implication in psychiatric disorders, this study aims to delineate the role of the heart-brain connection in the mechanisms underlying PTSD. While abnormal brain connectivity is central to PTSD, sympathetically-driven cardiac dysfunction also occurs. Given the established role of cardiac modulation of brain function and its implication in psychiatric disorders, this study aims to delineate the role of the heart-brain connection in the mechanisms underlying PTSD. METHODS: A single prolonged stress (SPS)-induced male C57BL/6J mouse model of PTSD was employed. Anxiety- and fear-like behaviors were assessed using behavioral tests. Cardiac function and insular cortex excitability were measured via ECG, in vivo electrophysiology, and immunofluorescence. Chronic isoproterenol (ISO) mimicked sympathetic cardiac overactivation. Left cervical vagotomy tested the cardiac-to-insular pathway. Propranolol's therapeutic effect was evaluated. A single prolonged stress (SPS)-induced male C57BL/6J mouse model of PTSD was employed.
